plastic.label: Plastic
instructions: Empty all liquid or food residue and rinse the item thoroughly.
Locate the recycling triangle number on the bottom or side of the package.
Most municipal collection programs accept resin codes #1 (PET) and #2 (HDPE) curbside.
Avoid placing plastic film, grocery bags, or lightweight wrappers into the curbside bin; these require a dedicated store drop-off point.

glass.label: Glass
instructions: Wash the container completely with water to remove food particles.
Detach metal lids or plastic caps, as they should be sorted separately.
Place clean bottles and jars in the recycling bin.
Note that broken glass, windows, mirrors, or drinking glasses are not curbside-safe and must be disposed of with general trash wrapped in thick paper.

paper_cardboard.label: Paper & Cardboard
instructions: Keep all paper products dry, as wet paper cannot be recycled.
Flatten cardboard boxes to save space in the bin.
Peel off excess adhesive packaging tape and remove large staples.
Dispose of heavily soiled items, like greasy pizza boxes or wax-coated cartons, in the general landfill container.

metal_cans.label: Metal Cans & Scrap
instructions: Empty food contents and rinse tin, steel, or aluminum cans.
If the metal lid is detached, push it inside the can or drop it in the bin separately.
Clean aluminum foil is recyclable if crumpled into a tight ball.
Take large scrap metal pieces to a local scrap yard or designated transfer facility rather than putting them curbside.

electronics_small.label: Small Electronics
instructions: Never throw electronic parts, cords, or chargers in the regular trash.
Gather any small cables, adapters, remote controls, or old headphones.
Locate a certified electronics retailer or supermarket that runs a free take-back program.
Alternatively, drop them off at your city's municipal e-waste recycling collection bin.

electronics_large.label: Large Electronics (laptop, TV, monitor)
instructions: Backup all personal records and wipe clean the storage drives on laptops or computers.
Contact the hardware manufacturer to see if they offer a free mail-back or recycling initiative.
Take bulky screens, monitors, printers, and televisions to a certified e-waste recycler.
Do not leave screens curbside without booking a specialty waste pickup.

batteries.label: Batteries
instructions: Put clear tape over the terminals of lithium or button-cell batteries to prevent electrical short-circuits.
Store spent batteries in a non-conductive plastic bucket until disposal.
Never toss batteries in curbside recycling or trash bins due to fire hazards.
Bring them to a battery collection tube found in hardware stores, grocery stores, or hazardous waste centers.

textile_clothing.label: Clothing & Textiles
instructions: Separate garments that are still in wearable condition from those that are torn or stained.
Launder the clothes to ensure they are clean and dry before disposal.
Donate wearable apparel to local charities or secondhand retail shops.
Drop heavily worn or shredded materials at a dedicated textile collection bin for industrial repurposing.

organic_food_waste.label: Food & Organic Waste
instructions: Collect fruit peels, vegetable scraps, coffee grounds, and eggshells.
Place organic waste in a backyard compost heap or a green curbside collection bin.
Avoid putting animal bones, meat leftovers, dairy products, or cooking oil into backyard composters to keep pests away.
Ensure no plastic stickers or bands are attached to produce skins.

hazardous_chemicals.label: Hazardous Chemicals (paint, oil, pesticide)
instructions: Keep paints, household chemicals, motor oil, and pesticides in their original packaging with legible labels.
Seal the lids tightly to prevent chemical spills or toxic vapor leaks.
Under no circumstances should you pour chemicals down a sink drain, storm drain, or onto the ground.
Search for your municipality's household hazardous waste (HHW) drop-off facility or special collection events.

light_bulbs_cfl.label: CFL / Fluorescent Bulbs
instructions: Handle CFL and fluorescent tubes carefully to avoid breaking them, as they contain trace amounts of mercury.
Pack the bulbs in a cardboard box or paper sleeve to cushion them.
Do not throw fluorescent bulbs into the general household trash.
Deliver them to participating home improvement stores or municipal hazardous waste collections.

furniture_large_items.label: Furniture & Bulky Items
instructions: Assess if the furniture can be repaired, repurposed, or donated to charity.
Check if your city's waste department offers bulk trash collection days.
Schedule a bulky item pickup service in advance if required.
Deliver large mattresses, couches, or wooden tables directly to a regional solid waste transfer station.

general_landfill.label: General Waste
instructions: Review municipal sorting guides to check if the material can be recycled locally.
If the item is mixed-material (like plastic bonded with metal or foil), it generally belongs in the landfill bin.
Bag the waste securely before tossing it into the gray/black general trash receptacle.
When in doubt, search the item name on your local waste management department's online directory.
